title: A Novel Proteomics Technology for Protein Farnesylation

key_phrases: The major goal of this application is to develop and validate a powerful technology, Tagging via Azido Substrate (TAS), for the efficient isolation of farnesylated proteins.

The resulting farnesyl-azide (F-azide)-modified proteins will be affinity-purified through an azide-specific conjugation reaction (Staudinger reaction) using a phosphine capture reagent linked to photo-cleavable beads, which can then be released by UV light-induced photo-cleavage.

The objectives of this SBIR Phase II are to develop a lead formulation of the contrast agent, to perform FDA required eIND enabling studies, and to commercialize the agent for clinical imaging prostate cancer.

The agent possesses the superior ability of robust specific contrast enhancement in high-risk prostate cancer, not in slow growing low-risk tumors in animal models, and has the potential to visualize and differentiate high-risk prostate cancers with MRI.
classification.reasoning: The project aims to develop a contrast agent for MRI to detect and differentiate high-risk prostate cancer, fitting the 'diagnostic' use. The research involves developing and testing a contrast agent in animal models (experimental_perturbation) and aims for clinical translation (implying future observational_retrospective data). The evidence for the biomarker's validity is currently preclinical, demonstrating its causal role in animal models.

title: Chromosomal Instability as a Therapeutic Target in Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer

key_phrases: We have also shown that ATC and poorly differentiated thyroid cancer (PDTC) tumors have high levels of critical SAC components, MPS1 and BUBR1.

We will test these hypotheses using in vitro human ATC/PDTC cells lines to elucidate mechanisms of synergy sensitivity and resistance, validate these findings in orthoptopic xenograft models, and extend these studies into syngeneic orthotopic models to determine the contribution of immunosurveillance on effectiveness of therapy.

Our central hypothesis is that the inhibition of the SAC pathway, responsible for the prevention of chromosomal segregation errors in ATC and PDTC cells, which already have high baseline chromosomal instability (CIN), will sensitize a vulnerable subset of tumor cells to taxanes and cause massive aneuploidy incompatible with cell survival.

we will use mass-cytometry to comprehensively characterize the hematopoietic hierarchy in healthy hematopoiesis, myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), and acute myeloid leukemia (AML), and how it is perturbed by treatment with the ciromicins and apoptolidins.

This approach has the potential to reconcile the observed selectivity of the apoptolidins with their reported mechanism which remains an open question in the field, and will provide the first in depth study of the mechanism of action of the ciromicins.
classification.reasoning: The project aims to classify cell subsets within hematopoietic disorders based on their response to specific compounds (ciromicins and apoptolidins), aligning with `stratification_diagnostic`. The research design involves perturbing cells with these compounds and characterizing the hematopoietic hierarchy, suggesting `experimental_perturbation` and `observational_cohort`. The validation of selectivity in cell types and the investigation of molecular mechanisms in preclinical models support `causal_preclinical` evidence strength.

title: Chromosomal Instability as a Therapeutic Target in Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer

key_phrases: We have also shown that ATC and poorly differentiated thyroid cancer (PDTC) tumors have high levels of critical SAC components, MPS1 and BUBR1.

We will test these hypotheses using in vitro human ATC/PDTC cells lines to elucidate mechanisms of synergy sensitivity and resistance, validate these findings in orthoptopic xenograft models, and extend these studies into syngeneic orthotopic models to determine the contribution of immunosurveillance on effectiveness of therapy.

Our central hypothesis is that the inhibition of the SAC pathway, responsible for the prevention of chromosomal segregation errors in ATC and PDTC cells, which already have high baseline chromosomal instability (CIN), will sensitize a vulnerable subset of tumor cells to taxanes and cause massive aneuploidy incompatible with cell survival.
classification.reasoning: The project aims to identify a subset of ATC patients who will benefit from a combination therapy (taxanes + SAC pathway inhibitors), suggesting a predictive role for SAC component levels. The research design involves in vitro and animal model experiments, and the evidence strength is preclinical as it validates the causal role of SAC pathway inhibition in sensitizing tumors to taxanes.

title: Prognostic Biomarkers for ZIP4-mediated Cachexia in Pancreatic Cancer

key_phrases: determine the prognostic value of ZIP4, identify ZIP4-activated catabolic mediators, and elucidate the mechanism(s) through which ZIP4 mediates muscle wasting in PC.

We will evaluate the prognostic values of ZIP4 and related catabolic pathway in PC by examining the correlation of ZIP4 in resected tumors with serum Hsp70/90 levels, p38 MAPK-activated catabolic pathway in muscles and clinical outcome (survival) of PC patients.

The central hypothesis of the proposed study is that ZIP4 mediates PC-induced muscle wasting through a distinctly enhanced activation of the p38 MAPK-mediated catabolic pathway by increasing expression and release of specific heat shock proteins and this ZIP4-enhanced cachexia pathway could serve as a prognostic marker for this dismal disease.
classification.reasoning: The project aims to identify prognostic markers for cachexia in pancreatic cancer patients, focusing on the correlation between ZIP4 levels and clinical outcomes like survival. While mouse models are used, the primary classification is prognostic risk due to the focus on predicting outcomes in established disease. The evidence strength is correlational as it focuses on associations and mechanisms rather than causal validation in humans.

title: Examining Biosynthesis and NAD+ Demand in the Warburg Effect

key_phrases: Our lab has recently developed a mathematical model of Warburg Effect, which indicates that Warburg Effect arises to help meet NAD+ demand.

title: Interplay between PDGFRA, oxygen-regulated translation and KSHV in Kaposi's sarcomagenesis

key_phrases: PDGFRA (+) mesenchymal stem cells as KS progenitors; and PDGFRA, as an enabler of KSHV oncogenesis

We will employ the MSC based de novo oncogenesis to tumorigenesis models, an induction reactivation model and two natural infections systems and AIDS-KS samples from different repositories to test these hypotheses.

title: Replication stress response defects predict and enhance immune checkpoint therapy response in triple negative breast cancer

key_phrases: RSRD signature score correlates perfectly with the response of TNBC to ICB in syngeneic mouse models, and it accurately predicts ICB response across 5 low?mutation-burden tumor lineages.

We will use a highly multiplexed imaging mass cytometry panel to determine how RSRD remodels the immune microenvironment of TNBC and induces susceptibility to ICB. In addition, we will manipulate the RSR status in TNBC cells to assess the relationship between RSR defects and immunotherapy response.
